event: Austronesian-speaking Lapita peoples voyaging from the west settle the Samoan archipelago, including the islands of Tutuila and Manuʻa.
Distinctive Lapita pottery is produced and used in early village settlements across the islands, marking the established Polynesian presence.
Samoa develops as a cultural and ancestral homeland from which later voyagers help settle other parts of central and eastern Polynesia.
The complex fa'a Samoa social system, centered on the matai (chiefly title) structure and extended aiga (family), matures across the islands.
The Manuʻa group emerges as a powerful chiefdom under the Tui Manuʻa, whose paramount sacred title commands wide prestige in the region.
Dutch explorer Jacob Roggeveen sights the Samoan islands, becoming the first European known to encounter the archipelago.
French navigator Louis-Antoine de Bougainville visits the islands and names them the Navigator Islands, impressed by Samoan seafaring.
A deadly clash between the expedition of French explorer La Perouse and Samoans at Tutuila's Aasu (Massacre Bay) leaves casualties on both sides.
Missionary John Williams of the London Missionary Society arrives, beginning the rapid Christianization of Samoa.
The United States Navy negotiates rights to a coaling and naval station at Pago Pago harbor on Tutuila with local chiefs.
The United States and Samoan chiefs formalize a treaty granting the U.S. exclusive use of Pago Pago harbor as a naval station.
A devastating cyclone wrecks American, German, and British warships anchored at Apia during a tense naval standoff over Samoa.
The Tripartite Convention partitions Samoa, granting the eastern islands (including Tutuila and Manuʻa) to the United States and the western islands to Germany.
Chiefs of Tutuila cede their islands to the United States in the Deed of Cession, placing them under U.S. Navy administration.
Commander Benjamin Tilley becomes the first U.S. naval commandant (governor) of American Samoa.
The chiefs of the Manuʻa Islands sign a Deed of Cession transferring their islands to the United States, completing American Samoa's territory.
Strict quarantine measures enforced by the naval governor spare American Samoa from the influenza pandemic that devastated nearby Western Samoa.
Swains Island, a privately owned atoll to the north, is formally annexed and added to American Samoa by act of the U.S. Congress.
The U.S. Congress ratifies the deeds of cession, formally accepting sovereignty over American Samoa.
The Mau movement, a peaceful campaign by Samoans for greater civil rights and self-government, gains strength under leaders such as Samuel Ripley.
Japanese submarine shelling of Tutuila during World War II prompts a major U.S. Marine buildup, transforming the territory into a key South Pacific base.
Administration of American Samoa is transferred from the U.S. Navy to the Department of the Interior, ending half a century of naval rule.
Peter Tali Coleman becomes the first native Samoan appointed governor of the territory.
American Samoa adopts its first constitution, establishing a bicameral legislature known as the Fono.
Governor H. Rex Lee launches a sweeping modernization program, including new schools, roads, and an educational television system.
An international airport and a new tuna cannery industry expand, with canneries becoming the territory's economic backbone.
A revised constitution takes effect, strengthening the powers of the locally elected Fono legislature.
Peter Tali Coleman wins the territory's first popular gubernatorial election, ending the era of appointed governors.
American Samoa sends its first elected nonvoting delegate to the U.S. House of Representatives.
Fofo Sunia is seated as American Samoa's first delegate to Congress, beginning continuous representation in Washington.
Eni Faleomavaega begins a long tenure as the territory's congressional delegate, advocating for veterans and Pacific issues.
Tropical Cyclone Ofa causes severe damage across American Samoa, devastating crops and infrastructure.
Cyclone Val strikes the islands, compounding storm damage and prompting major U.S. disaster relief efforts.
The National Park of American Samoa is established, protecting rainforest, reefs, and Samoan cultural sites across three islands.
Togiola Tulafono becomes governor after the death of Governor Tauese Sunia, later winning election in his own right.
A powerful undersea earthquake triggers a tsunami that kills dozens and causes widespread destruction along Tutuila's coast.
Aumua Amata Coleman Radewagen takes office as American Samoa's first woman elected to Congress as its delegate.
A federal court ruling and ongoing debate highlight the unique status of American Samoans, who are U.S. nationals but not automatically U.S. citizens.
Tropical Cyclone Gita causes flooding and damage, underscoring the islands' vulnerability to intensifying Pacific storms.
Sea-level rise and coral reef decline driven by climate change increasingly threaten low-lying coastal villages and subsistence livelihoods.
American Samoa continues to grapple with economic reliance on tuna canneries, U.S. federal funding, and the challenges of climate adaptation.
